segments.text: Hello there! I want to make sure you have a good time and that you don't get stuck
on anything relating to connecting to our lab environment. Some of the material
you're going to be learning is tricky enough, so you'll have enough on your
mind with just that. Let's make sure that logging into our system and navigating
around is as smooth and painless as possible. You'll be logging into an
actual IBM Z system, and this is separate from whatever learning platform you're
on. To get you connected will also be giving you access to a virtual Windows
system, and that has all of the connection software pre-loaded on it,
and you'll use that to connect to the mainframe system. This is the easiest way
to get you connected without having you to install all sorts of software on your
own machine. When you go to connect to the lab system, you'll eventually see a
loading screen that looks something like this, and then something like this, and
then something like this, and then eventually you'll get to a screen that
looks something like this with the green box and the course lab kit up here. This
is your connection to that system, and there's three important things on here
that I want to show you. First is the lab kit up top here. That link wasn't there
until the system was ready. This is a document made just for you, and it has
your connection information in it. You'll need that information, so maybe
bookmark it or print it out so you can find it easily later on. This page also
shows you the amount of time left. Now, this is not the amount of time left in
the course. Remember, two separate things. This is the amount of time left on the
hands-on system, and if you need more time to get your work done on the system,
go down to support. There's a link in your course lab kit, and then visit the
self-service portal to request an extension, and just so you know, the time
to do that is before you need that extra time, and the sooner the better. Trust me
on this, you don't want to wait until the last minute, or they might not be able to
change it in time, and you might lose all of your work on that system, so pay
attention to that date. Alright, you got that. Last thing is your virtual system.
Access. This is that virtual Windows system that I talked about earlier, and
you'll use this to connect to the IBM Z mainframe system. This will normally say
running, but if you ever come in here after a while and it says busy, then you
probably just have to wait a little while for it to come back online. No big
deal. This is also a good time to point out that sometimes you'll see values on
your screen that are just examples. Things like user IDs and high-level
qualifiers. Now eventually you'll be able to spot those right away, no problem,
but when you see something that maybe looks like your user ID but isn't
actually your user ID, you're probably expected to put in your user ID, not the
one you see on the screen. You wouldn't believe how often someone copies and
pastes a command exactly as it is into the system, and then they get really
frustrated when it doesn't work. So double check the examples, double check
the instructions, and make sure you're entering what you're supposed to enter.
To get into this system you'll need a username and password, and I can't tell
it to you and we can't make it log in automatically. The username and password
are in your course lab kit. Remember that link up at the top of the screen? Now
there are two usernames and passwords in here. The first one is for getting into
that Windows system. It's in the remote access information section. Then below
that in lab information you'll find these student TSO user IDs, and it might
just be one, it might be two, it'll probably just be one, but you'll use
those credentials to log into the mainframe system which we'll cover in the
next video. I hope that helps you get off to a great start with the labs in
your course. Have fun and learn a lot.
